coredata.dc:title: Chapter 8 Microfluidic production of inorganic nanomaterials for biomedical applications

Arruebo, Manuel
coredata.dc:description: Microfluidic flow reactors can speed up the scale-up production of inorganic nanomaterials and allow understanding the influence of different independent synthesis variables in the physicochemical characteristics of the resulting products. They can also be used not only for a controlled synthesis and an in-line functionalization but also to easily modify the synthesis parameters and the consequent physicochemical characteristics of the resulting products with feedback control. In the biomedical field as carriers of bioactive molecules, inorganic nanoparticles (NPs) have shown an improved solubility and amended pharmacokinetics compared with the administration of the free active principle. In the field of medical imaging, all the noninvasive techniques can be enhanced by using inorganic nanomaterials as contrast agents allowing an early diagnosis and staging of disease. Early detection of pathogenic microorganisms or different analytes at very low limits of detection can also be achieved by using inorganic NPs as transducers in biosensors. Tissue engineering and cancer are also two medical fields, which benefit from the use of inorganic NPs to augment tissue regeneration and to act as electromagnetic absorbers to generate ablation, respectively.

Main advantages and disadvantages of single- and multiple-phase flow patterns used in the continuous production of nanomaterials

row.entry:
- Flexible operation
- Easy control
- Versatile production of nanomaterials

- Slow mixing
- Not applicable in fast kinetically controlled reactions
- Wide residence time distribution
- Prone to particle deposition
- Channel clogging

- Rapid mixing
- Reduced residence time distribution
- Facile separation of gas/liquid slugs
- Applicable in fast kinetically controlled reactions

- Difficult sequential addition of reagents
- Particle deposition
- Channel clogging

- Rapid mixing
- Reduced residence time distribution
- Long operation times
- Preserve from fouling
- Applicable in fast kinetically controlled reactions

- Difficult sequential addition of reagents
- Additional stage to separate the immiscible fluids
- Partial solubility of reagents in both liquid phases

chapter.ce:title: Microfluidic production of inorganic nanomaterials for biomedical applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microfluidic flow reactors can speed up the scale-up production of inorganic nanomaterials and allow understanding the influence of different independent synthesis variables in the physicochemical characteristics of the resulting products. They can also be used not only for a controlled synthesis and an in-line functionalization but also to easily modify the synthesis parameters and the consequent physicochemical characteristics of the resulting products with feedback control.

In the biomedical field as carriers of bioactive molecules, inorganic nanoparticles (NPs) have shown an improved solubility and amended pharmacokinetics compared with the administration of the free active principle. In the field of medical imaging, all the noninvasive techniques can be enhanced by using inorganic nanomaterials as contrast agents allowing an early diagnosis and staging of disease. Early detection of pathogenic microorganisms or different analytes at very low limits of detection can also be achieved by using inorganic NPs as transducers in biosensors. Tissue engineering and cancer are also two medical fields, which benefit from the use of inorganic NPs to augment tissue regeneration and to act as electromagnetic absorbers to generate ablation, respectively.

Microfluidic Based Synthesis of Inorganic Nanoparticles With Potential Use in Biomedicine 193

ce:para.ce:bold: Biomedical Applications of Inorganic Nanomaterials Produced by Microfluidics

ce:list-item.ce:para: Inorganic Nanoparticles in Magnetic Fluid Hyperthermia and in Photothermal Therapy 205

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last decades, significant progress has been made in the development of nanotechnological approaches to diagnose, screen, treat, and prevent disease improving human health. As a consequence of this effort, 43 nanomedicines have been approved for the clinical practice by the European Medicines Agency (EMA) and 71 by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) mainly as drugs and medical devices, and other 128 are currently undergoing clinical trials [1,2]. Currently, corporate R&D departments, private foundations, and governmental research councils are supporting a plethora of new research projects and clinical trials focused on nanotechnology. Thus, the fiscal year 2016 funding request in the United States for nanotechnology research and development was $1.5 billion being the field of health care through all its national federal agencies the one with the largest funding request at $448.6 million [3]. In Europe, financial support is directed toward projects with a strong translational focus from preclinical studies to clinical trials [4]. As a reference, the European Commission dedicated approximately 108M€ for the topics of the 2016–17 call devoted to advanced materials and nanotechnologies for health care out of a total of 1078M€ of the call for nanotechnologies, advanced materials, biotechnology, and production (call located within the Pillar 2, support for “Industrial Leadership,” of the Horizon 2020 program [5]).

All this economical support is based on the demonstrated benefits of nanomaterials in the biomedical field thanks to their application principally as drug delivery systems in cancer treatment and in antimicrobial therapy. Nanomaterials applied in medical imaging techniques and in the development of advanced biosensors enable also an enhanced performance improving clinical outcomes by addressing some of the shortcomings of the conventional diagnosis techniques by interacting selectively with different biomolecules at molecular scale. Regenerative medicine is also benefiting from the development of new nanobiomaterials and new tissue-engineering substitutes that speed up and closely mimic the natural healing process.

Fig. 8.1 describes the distinct areas of health care in which nanomaterials are demonstrating clinical progress. As carriers of drugs, genes, or other bioactive molecules, NPs have shown an improved solubility and amended pharmacokinetics compared with the administration of the free drug [6]. The conjugation of poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) to biomolecules and drugs or to NPs was early introduced in the late 1960s [7] with the purpose of enhancing the drug or carrier circulation lifetime, protecting the cargo against enzymatic degradation, and avoiding a rapid clearance by the reticuloendothelial system. By coating therapeutic or diagnostic NPs with PEG, the first response of the immune system to a foreign body, which is opsonin adsorption, is reduced, and consequently, the PEGylated NPs gain circulation lifetime extravasating and passively reaching the target tissues. This passive targeting is especially important in cancer treatment where a proper NP design allows an overaccumulation of the drug-loaded NPs in the tumoral tissue thanks to the enhanced permeation and retention (EPR) effect and to the poor lymphatic drainage of solid tumors. However, only a 1–5% of the injected bioavailable systemic dose accumulates in the tumor [8,9], and limited experimental results from patients [10] drive the need of designing more selective NPs and also developing theragnostic NPs where the medical outcome can be correlated with imaging techniques that will allow in the near future to understand the complex and heterogeneous EPR effect. Also theragnostic NPs can help to unravel the drug delivery process and all the cells involved. In this regard, by using theragnostic NPs, Miller et al. [11] showed how tumor-associated macrophages are beneficial by accumulating nanoparticulate drugs and acting as slow-releasing systems.

Despite all the advances reached in the field of active targeted drug delivery systems by using NPs functionalized with homing molecules (i.e., antibodies, aptamers, and peptides), being some of them even in clinical trials [12,13], or by using homing cells as cargos transporting therapeutic NPs (i.e., mesenchymal stem cells [14] and lymphocytes [15]), still, immune recognition, demonstrated efficacy, and large-scale production are the main hurdles to overcome when using this active targeting approach [16].

Drug delivery on demand can also be achievable by using NPs that respond to a physical (light, ultrasounds, magnetic or electric fields, etc.) or pathophysiological stimuli (i.e., acidic environment, enzyme or protein presence, and redox variations) [17]. The initial designs were systems that unloaded all the active principle contained in the nanocarrier in response to the stimuli, but further developments led to the design of reversible stimuli-responsive nanomaterials that allow switching on and off the release of the contained molecule of interest. In this regard, azobenzenes and spiropyrans have been widely used as photochemical hydrophobicity switches to trigger the release of the active principle on demand [18,19].

In the field of medical imaging, all the noninvasive techniques used including magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), positron emission tomography (PET), computerized axial tomography (CAT), ultrasounds, and optical imaging can be enhanced by using nanomaterials as contrast agents to allow an early diagnosis and staging of disease. In addition, multimodal imaging can be obtained by using multifunctional NPs aiding in a more accurate diagnosis of the disease [20,21]. Besides the great advances reached in preclinical models, still, safety assessment, demonstrated benefit over the current technologies, and large-scale production deserve further development. As an example, FDA-approved superparamagnetic iron oxide NPs (SPIONs) were used as MRI contrast agents to image liver lesions (i.e., Feridex), but they have been discontinued due to safety or market issues [22].

Cost-efficient, sensitive, and selective biosensors can be designed incorporating NPs as enhancers of the physicochemical signal used for the analyte detection or transduction. Point-of-care diagnostics is also an area of research that is benefiting from the incorporation of nanomaterials reaching even commercial and clinical translation [23]. Multianalyte and high-throughput detections are a reality nowadays thanks to the development of nanotechnological approaches, and a considerable increase in research in those fields will be made in the coming years. Several other examples of commonly used inorganic NPs functionalized with biomolecules for applications in biomedicine are reviewed elsewhere [24].

Nanomaterials have been widely used in soft tissue engineering including neural, cardiac, and cartilage tissue engineering and also in hard tissues including bone, dental, and craniofacial tissue engineering. In this regard, nanostructured materials (i.e., electrospun nanofibers) are used as scaffolding materials to mimic the 3-D extracellular matrix. Their high surface-area-to-volume ratio favors the interactions between the regenerative cells during healing. Those interactions can be tuned depending on the surface nanotopography and have allowed, thanks to the advances in lithographic techniques learned from the semiconductor industry, the manipulation of cell morphology, signaling, orientation, adhesion, migration, proliferation, and differentiation [25]. In regenerative medicine, nanoparticles can also be used as beacons to label transplanted stem cells to follow the integration process by MRI [26], by photoacoustic imaging [27], by multimodal imaging techniques [28], etc. In cell therapy, NPs internalized within the transplanted cells (i.e., stem cells, chondrocytes, and osteoblasts) can also be used as cargos of adjuvant drugs to maximize cell efficacy and in vivo cell persistence avoiding the need of a systemic administration of the adjuvant drug [29].

In this field, some nanomaterials can act as inductors of new forming bone. In this regard, nanohydroxyapatite combines not only the inductive ability that apatite has to promote bone regeneration, due to their compositional similarities with the bone, but also its nanoscale, which favors a seeded growth during bone formation [30]. Also those nanomaterials can be added as nanofillers to other polymeric matrices used as scaffolds to increase their mechanical properties needed to bear the mechanical loads [31,32]. Nanosilver is also broadly used to prevent infection associated with the surgical or implantation procedures, and several silver-doped dressings [33], retainers [34], scaffolds [35], and coatings on prosthesis [36] have shown bacterial eradication. Carbon-based materials including carbon nanotubes, graphene, nanodiamond, and other allotropic forms have also been used as fillers in tissue engineering taking advantage of their outstanding mechanical, electric, and optical properties [37,38].

Nanomaterials as depots of bioactive substances grafted or embedded in natural or synthetic scaffolds allow a spatiotemporal release of bioactive molecules to promote vascularization, regeneration, and differentiation. For instance, self-assembled heparin/chitosan NPs loaded with vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) showed an accelerated vascularization in mouse subcutaneous implantation model in vivo [39]. A spatiotemporal expression of transgenic VEGF is also achievable noninvasively in vivo by using thermally responsible NPs embedded within fibrin hydrogels [40]. To prevent an uncontrolled release of growth factors from the doped scaffolds, the nanoencapsulation of differentiation-inducing molecules within NPs has been also proven as an efficient and persistent chondrogenetic strategy [41]. All those strategies are designed to mimic the surface properties of the changing microenvironments where the scaffold is placed during the regenerative process.

In line with the progressing interest of biomedicine in the design of advanced nanostructured materials, the controlled synthesis of nanometer-sized structured materials is challenging. Microfluidic reactors have demonstrated successful results in fast experimental optimization parameters screening for nanomaterial synthesis [42]. This section provides an overview of the adoption of microfluidic flow chemistry in the synthesis of inorganic NPs for biomedical use. Microfluidics allows a novel process control window of well-defined NPs, where the nanomaterials produced in conventional batch-type reactors are replicated but with an excellent control in size distribution, shape, and composition.

Conventional flask-based reactors are usually selected as the adequate reactor to prepare NPs both at lab scale and to scale up the production. However, flask- or batch-type reactors suffer from serious limitations to control the production of nanomaterials at the molecular level. These limitations are originated by the inefficient heat and mass transfer that unable a good control in the synthesis conditions. The lack of reaction control is even more important in fast kinetic reactions, where the reaction time can be comparable with the mixing time. A nonhomogeneous mixing of reagents will generate concentration gradients in the reaction volume that can alter the nucleation rate, producing a lack of synchronization between the nuclei formation and growth [43]. This phenomenon will promote precursor competition between two parallel processes: new nucleation and growth of the formed nuclei, which are responsible for polydispersity in size, shape, and chemical composition. Then, it is accepted that nucleation and growth stages should be separated in time to facilitate monodisperse NP production. This scenario is challenging to be achieved in a controlled manner in conventional stirred reactors (typically flasks), where the characteristic mixing time is relatively long as compared with the nucleation time [43]. As a result, synthesis of nanomaterials in conventional batch reactors suffers from significant batch-to-batch variability.

Scaling up the production of inorganic NPs while retaining accurate control on their properties has been identified as one of the main barriers for the development of nanotechnology [44]. Nucleation and growth are highly sensitive to temperature and to the way that reagents are introduced and mixed. These parameters are sensitive to the reactor volume, and consequently, the control acquired at the lab scale is different to the one achieved at the mass-scale production. Due to the aforementioned limitations during the NP production, there is often a trade-off between maintaining the ability to control the desired nanoscale features and achieving high production rates [44].

The production of hollow gold nanoparticles (HGNPs) in conventional batch-type reactors constitutes a good example of the need for strict dimensional control. The application of this type of NPs in biomedicine requires to have a surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak in the near-infrared (NIR) region ranging from 700 to 900nm. This wavelength range is the so-called transparent window or water window, and light can penetrate deeply into tissues due to the reduction in light absorption from hemoglobin, melanin, and water present in the blood [45]. The synthesis of HGNPs requires the production of Co NPs as sacrificial templates. Then, based on the galvanic replacement reaction, the addition of Au3+ ions to oxidize cobalt results in the deposition of Au0 over the cobalt surface and formation of Co2+ ions (further information about galvanic replacement reaction will be given in Section 2.5.1). This chemical procedure generates a gold shell, and the removal of cobalt eventually produces a hollow structure inside a rough Au shell decorated with nanopores [46]. In these hollow structures, the wall thickness and the morphology uniformity determine the SPR peak position and the SPR bandwidth, respectively. Considering these facts and the reported data [47], we decided to scale up the productivity of HGNPs by two approaches [46] (Fig. 8.2 A): (1) increasing the concentration of reagents and (2) increasing the reactor volume.

Fig. 8.2A depicts the HGNPs produced when the concentration of reagents was increased by six times, maintaining the reactor volume constant, to increase the productivity in the same order. The resulting NPs were heterogeneous in size and shape, and the wall thickness was not controlled. These facts clearly affected the optical properties of HGNPs and hindered any potential use in biomedical applications. The lack of reproducibility in the scaled-up production of HGNPs by concentration increase was caused by a mismatch between the reaction and mixing times. Increasing the reagent concentration resulted in a faster growth kinetics that hampered the proper reagent mixing. In this case, gold ions were fast replacing the Co atoms in the proximity of the gold precursor addition, promoting the formation of large particles having a thick wall (Fig. 8.2B and C), whereas the replacement was slow in the rest of the colloid-producing HGNPs with a thin wall. Consequently, the mixing time must be usually shorter than the reaction time in order to control the nanocrystallization process. This main cause hinders the use of batch-type reactors in fast reactions.

On the other hand, if the HGNP productivity were faced by increasing one order of magnitude the reactor volume from the reported procedure (120mL) to 1200mL, a lack of control in the morphology and shape of the resulting NPs (Fig. 8.2D–H) and in their optical properties (Fig. 8.2I) was observed. The quality of the HGNPs was only maintained if the reaction volume was increased up to 480mL. In this case, the reaction kinetics were not modified because the reagent concentration was similar to the reported procedure. However, the mixing time and thermal transport were modified since they do not scale linearly with the volume of the reactor [46].

It is of paramount importance to consider new alternatives for nanomaterial production considering that conventional reactors are not appropriate for nanomaterial production and that the properties that we demand from nanomaterials are critically dependent on their size, shape, chemical composition, surface characteristics, and degree of aggregation.

During the last decade, microscale reactors have been considered as a versatile alternative to produce a wide variety of nanomaterials. A reduction in reaction volume ensures better thermal and compositional uniformity, leading to improved synthesis control. In addition, microfluidic reactors benefit from rapid mixing, fast heating as the surface-to-volume ratio increases, excellent control of nucleation and growth stages, good flexibility to carry out multistep processes, in-line analysis, and good reproducibility. Inspired by these advantages, microfluidic technology has been developed to produce a plethora of inorganic NPs with biomedical applications. These nanomaterials will be conveniently described in Section 2.4 of this chapter.

Considering that a microreactor is a micro vessel designed to contain chemical reactions, its design should optimize the reactor structure to improve the efficiency of mass and heat transfer on a specific nanocrystallization reaction. These requirements should direct the synthesis to achieve the highest yield to the desired particles with a tunable shape, size, and composition, as well as a homogenous particle size distribution. Microreactor systems for nanomaterial production can be composed of a large number of components, which include pumps for delivering reactants, feedback-controlled heater/cooling units, valves, back-pressure regulators, sampling valves, and in-line chemical analysis equipment [48]. The type of microreactor utilized for synthesizing NPs should be selected under a function-relevant criterion, such as corrosion resistance, wettability, mechanical strength, temperature range in which the chemical reaction occurs, price, availability, and workability. Nevertheless, no single material fulfills all these criteria to a completely ideal extent, and it means that a compromise is usually required between the most important variables [49].

Different types of microreactors have been utilized in the production of nanomaterials. They have evolved from simple capillary tubing (Fig. 8.3 A) to complex design integrating valves, control of operation parameters, and in situ characterization, appropriately named “lab-on-a-chip” [49]. Capillary tubing microfluidic reactors are widely used due to their simplicity in microfluidic design and fabrication, which enable to achieve an easy scalability of the process and adjustment of the resident time by just modifying the tubing length. However, the quality of NPs produced in this type of microreactor is compromised with a slow mixing or wide particle size distributions. The most commonly used capillary microfluidic reactors are made of polymers due to the fact that these microreactors are easy to fabricate at a low cost and with an acceptable feature resolution (i.e., 25μm) [49]. Polymer tubing made of transparent or semitransparent materials eases the optical access while the nanocrystallization reaction is produced, which is convenient to follow the reaction in advance and observe reactor fouling [49]. Among the polymeric tubes, those made of PTFE are the most widely used since they can tolerate a broad range of chemicals and elevated temperatures (up to 240°C). In addition, as a result of the hydrophobic nature of PTFE, it naturally withstands channel fouling and blockage when aqueous streams are injected in the flow reactor [49,50]. On the other hand, metal or glass capillaries are preferred to carry out the production of NPs at high-temperature and high-pressure conditions.

An alternative to capillary microfluidic reactors is the use of microfluidic chips fabricated using soft-lithography techniques. The most commonly fabricated microsystems are made of poly(dimethyl siloxane) (PDMS) and metal and silicon oxide [49]. The feature resolution of this type of microreactors (submicron) is higher than that obtained in the capillary ones and enables to introduce a wide variety of subunits in the chip design: multiple inlet ports, flow distributors, mixing units, reaction units, separation units, cooling/heating microchannels, in-line analysis, valves, multiple reagent addition, and pressure/temperature sensors. PDMS microsystems have a limited operating temperature and pressure and a poor chemical compatibility, since PDMS is swelled by most of the organic solvents; however, it has been widely reported in the synthesis of some inorganic NPs. Some microreactors made of SU-8 [51] or PEEK [52] have been reported to withstand temperatures and pressures up to 150°C and 2MPa, respectively. For instance, Fig. 8.3B illustrates a PEEK interdigital micromixer that enables to achieve fast mixing of reagents in the millisecond scale [52].

Silicon chip microreactors are also fabricated by conventional lithographic techniques, and they have the advantage of high bond strengths, which favor their use in high-temperature and high-pressure conditions. This type of reactor can be used with any type of solvent, but their use is not recommended in high-alkaline solutions because the hydroxyl anions can etch the microchannels and modify the microreactor fluid dynamics. A silicon-Pyrex chip microreactor was designed to produce a wide variety of noble metal nanoparticles by tuning the synthesis conditions and flow type (Fig. 8.3C) [53]. The reagents were fast mixed in the meandering channels at room temperature and were introduced in the spiral reaction microchannel to promote a homogenous nucleation and controlled growth. A continuous-flow silicon-based microsystem structured in three stages (mixing, aging, and sequential addition of reagents) was designed to enable a fine control in the InP nanocrystal production [54]. This microsystem can operate in a continuous mode at 65bar and a temperature as high as 340°C. In addition, the microsystem was able to screen the most important parameters to control the growth of InP nanocrystals, that is, optical properties were sensitive to particle size distribution and chemical composition without any manual batch manipulation between synthesis steps [54] (further information about InP nanocrystals will be given in Section 2.5). The design of microchannels in this type of multistage microsystems requires the presence of pressure drop zones with high flow resistance to obtain a uniform flow and to prevent backflow.

Lithographic techniques are precise enough to produce 3-D microchannels to interface several streams, achieving a rapid and efficient mixing. This type of microchannel arrangement is required in fast reactions and can be achieved by lamination or by chaotic advection. Fig. 8.3E shows a glass micromixer designed for millisecond mixing of two fluid streams by lamination. In this case, the fast mixing is achieved by increasing the contact surface between two streams that are split and joined to decrease the diffusional pathway. This type of glass or silicon-Pyrex microreactor has the advantage of easy optical access to enable the in-line analysis and the visualization of fouling. However, the blockage of microfluidic channels caused either by nanoparticle agglomeration or by nucleation in the microchannel walls is a major concern because this cannot be reused once the microreactor is blocked. In addition, glass or silicon microreactors are fragile and do not withstand mechanical impacts.

Metal-based microreactors are designed to hamper the fragility of silicon/glass microreactors (Fig. 8.3D). Metal-based microreactors are also recommended for process involving high heat transfer, except for the treatment of low-pH reagents where the metal can be etched. A limitation of using metal-based microreactors is the presence of leaks at the joints, specially working at high pressure. However, this drawback can be faced by welding or soldering processes that enable to achieve high-strength intermetallic bonds [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production of Nanomaterials by Continuous Flow Approaches: Continuous, Segmented and Oscillatory Flow
ce:section.ce:para: Inorganic nanoparticles with a target application in biomedicine are usually produced in microfluidics according to wet-chemistry methodology. Wet-chemical procedures are considered as one of the most convenient techniques for producing nanostructures with an accurate control on the dimensions, morphology, and composition [49]. Inorganic NPs can be produced with subnanometer-level accuracy by careful regulation of crystallization kinetics and thermodynamic parameters, such as temperature and reagent concentration, under the assistance of selected solvents, ligands, and surfactants. Wet-chemical procedures can be divided into hydrothermal and solvothermal synthesis; microemulsions; thermal decomposition; sol-gel methods; and photochemical, sonochemical, and electrochemical processes.

The main wet-chemistry synthesis strategies carried out in microfluidic reactors can be mainly grouped into two categories depending on their flow patterns: single-phase and multiphase flow. Liquid flow within microchannels is strictly a laminar flow due to the prevalence of viscous forces over inertial forces. In single-phase laminar flow, the fluid velocity distribution depends on the boundary conditions, resulting in a parabolic distribution of fluid velocity (Fig. 8.4 A). In addition, the absence of turbulence results in a mass transfer based purely on molecular diffusion. These two fluid dynamic factors can result in [56] Taylor dispersion, solute-surface interactions, cross contamination, and the need for substantial volumes of reagents and relatively long channel lengths. On the other hand, single-phase flow does not require the use of complex devices to assure a steady production of nanomaterials. In fact, a wide variety of inorganic nanostructures can be produced in single-phase flow [53] if the proper synthesis conditions are achieved to (1) avoid microchannel fouling and (2) reduce the residence time distribution promoting a fast mixing with a passive mixing microchannel layout (Fig. 8.4B). However, single-phase flow is not recommended in fast kinetic reactions. Single-phase flow can be implemented in a coaxial-flow reactor (Fig. 8.4C), which allows an inner stream of reagents to be surrounded by an outer stream, creating a reaction interface between the streams. This microchannel layout can mitigate the fouling process in the reactor walls because the reduction of precursor ions and nucleation of NPs occur at the interface between the inner and outer streams, rather than near the microchannel walls [57].

Multiphase flow patterns, such as gas-liquid and liquid-liquid, are interesting alternatives to address the limitations of single-phase flow microreactors. The dominant interfacial forces combined with the laminar nature of this flow pattern lead to temporal variations in properties such as pressure drop or concentration of the reagents contained in one of the fluid phases. A variety of flow regimes can be classified into multiphase flow: bubbly, slug/segmented, churn, annular, etc. [58]. Slug or segmented flow is usually more appropriate to control the production of nanomaterials, because its dynamic behavior and the recirculation motion localized inside the liquid segments enable to enhance the mixing of liquid streams (Fig. 8.4D and E). Then, this flow pattern can overcome the transport limitations in single-phase microflows [58]. However, the internal circulation must be tuned in order to produce monodisperse NPs in fast kinetic nanocrystallization reactions. Although the reduction of axial dispersion and residence time distribution is considerable in comparison with single-phase flow pattern (Fig. 8.4A), the slip velocity between the two fluids and the internal mixing in the continuous-phase slugs govern the narrowness of the particle size distribution [59]. In addition, it is challenging in multiphase microsystems to add reagents along the entire length of the channel without the need of synchronizing the dropping frequency.

Finally, oscillatory multiphase flow is an alternative flow pattern to achieve decoupling of mixing and residence times, and consequently, this pattern could afford achieving long processing times >10min. [60]. Consequently, this approach is especially recommended for slow chemical reactions and mass-transfer-limited processes. Since the length of the microchannel is limited by the pressure drop, the simple back-and-forth motion of slugs enables tuning on demand the residence time (Fig. 8.4F). In addition, a single-point temporal characterization of the process of interest within the microreaction vessel can be achieved without the need for manual sampling or formation of single droplets per reaction time [60]. Table 8.1 summarizes the main advantages and disadvantages of single- and multiple-phase flow patterns used in the continuous production of nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computer Fluid Dynamic Simulations, A Key Tool in Microfluidic Systems
ce:section.ce:para: Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) has emerged as an effective tool to provide visualized information about the complex fluid phenomena that occur in microfluidic devices. Considering the dimensions of microfluidic reactors and the complex distribution of fluids at the microscale, it is clear that CFD is a key tool to design microsystems and understand a plethora of chemical and physical processes that simultaneously occur in microsystems. Achieving the optimum design of microsystems is very important to save fabrication resources, and with this objective in mind, preprocessing steps such as a mathematical simulation can be very helpful. CFD is especially interesting to get insights about the fluid dynamics of micromixers, where vortices’ phenomena, fluid mixing mechanisms, and mixing efficiency must be studied in a wide range of working conditions [61] (Fig. 8.5 A). Successful mixing of fluid streams through microchannels is not as easy as in macroscale systems because viscous effects can dominate the fluid dynamics and hamper rapid mixing. As it was aforementioned in Section 2.1, effective mixing is essential in nanomaterial production because it will determine if the mixing time was smaller than the reaction time in order to produce a homogenous control in nanocrystal size, shape, and composition during the growth (Fig. 8.5B and C). The fluid dynamics at the microchannels can be determined by solving the following three equations: the continuity equation, the Navier-Stokes equations, and the convection-diffusion equation, along with the appropriate boundary conditions [62]. It is of paramount importance to select the proper spatial discretization of the microsystem in order to balance the model accuracy and to reduce the computation time. Diffusion-reaction models have been considered to describe the fluid-flow behaviors in microchannels where a nucleation-growth process occurs to produce nanomaterials [63], but this approach is challenging because the nanocrystallization kinetics is practically unknown and there is a lack of appropriate reaction kinetic data [64].

Along with numerical modeling, microscale flow visualization plays an important role to understand microfluidic flows in processes occurring at the microscale. Regular fluorophores such as fluorescein are commonly used for the direct visualization of mixing in microfluidic devices, obtaining high spatial resolution by the use of confocal laser scanning microscopy [65]. The microscopic particle image velocimetry (PIV) has been successfully applied in segmented flow microreactors where a fast mixing was required to control the growth of Au NPs. PIV enabled to determine the internal circulation of the dispersed phase and the recirculation zone of the continuous phase to validate experimental results (Fig. 8.4D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microfluidic Based Synthesis of Inorganic Nanoparticles With Potential Use in Biomedicine
ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, there is a tremendous interest in the development of microfluidic procedures for synthesizing inorganic nanomaterial with a potential use in biomedicine. As it was noted in Section 2.1, batch synthesis often suffers from limited reproducibility to control size distribution, shape, and chemical composition. Consequently, the inherent advantages of using microfluidics and the fast screening of proper synthesis conditions under low reagent consume make microfluidics to be considered as an essential tool in pharmaceutical and fine chemical industries [48]. There are numerous examples of synthesis of inorganic NPs in continuous flow that can be applied in biomedicine [42,49,53,66,67]. Nevertheless, in this section, the most relevant examples in terms of size, shape, and chemical composition control will be summarized, because these variables will decide if the nanomaterial properties are eligible for a biomedical use or not.

Fig. 8.6 summarizes the most important nanomaterials produced in a continuous fashion with a potential interest in biomedical applications. These nanomaterials can be classified into noble metal, semiconductors, and oxides.

Regarding noble metal NPs, gold NPs are the most well-stablished nanostructures in biomedical applications since their optical properties can be tuned by just modifying their size and shape. In addition, the gold chemistry has been extensively studied, and there are a wide variety of well-stablished functionalization procedures. Since Turkevich et al. [68] stablished one of the most frequently used synthetic routes, a plethora of synthetic approaches has been designed. Spherical gold nanoparticles have been produced using mild or strong reducing agents, but the initial reduction of the gold precursor into the gold nuclei is a fast process (<200ms [59]), and an efficient mixing previous to the nucleation stage is required to control the nanoparticle size distribution. As it was previously noted in Section 2.3, single-phase flow is not recommended for fast kinetic reactions due to the axial dispersion generated in a laminar flow having a parabolic velocity profile. Then, segmented flow is usually recommended to achieve a fast mixing. However, a careful choice of continuous and dispersed phases must be achieved. Au NP size distribution is primarily governed by the slip velocity between the two fluids in the segmented flow and the internal mixing in the slugs [59]. Considering these facts, it was observed, in this case, that the NP size distribution was the narrowest if air were used as the continuous phase instead of oil or toluene. In addition, the NP size distribution was not well controlled at low flow rates because the slug length was the highest and the interslug mass transfer was not well achieved [59].

Anisotropic growth of Au NPs into rod shape has been successfully achieved in microfluidic reactors by a secondary growth method where Au seeds (nuclei) are first prepared and then some crystal faces {110} are preferentially capped with ligands to direct the synthesis toward the rod shape [69]. However, this method is sensitive to nuclei aging, and depending on the aging time, the resulting optical properties (SPR) can vary significantly [70]. Microfluidics enables producing nanomaterials by a multistage process, which exerts the advantage of hampering the aforementioned issue. Then, nuclei can be first synthesized and then anisotropically grown into a nanorod shape just by connecting different microfluidic units. In addition, new stages can be added, for instance, gold nanorod functionalization with PEG. Fig. 8.7 B shows PEG-Au nanorods produced with a residence time of 32min in a continuous-flow platform that comprises four stages (Fig. 8.7A): (1) Au precursor activation, (2) Au seed formation, (3) Au nanorod growth, and (4) Au nanorod pegylation [70]. The productivity of this type of microfluidic systems can be increased by the use of millireactors, where a fine control of nanorod aspect ratio (1:4) can be achieved under a productivity of grams per hour [71]. Finally, previous syntheses rely in the use of hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) to control the anisotropic growth. This ligand is highly toxic for many biomedical applications, and it should be eliminated or exchanged by some biocompatible ligand such as PEG [72]. To circumvent CTAB toxicity, a novel procedure based on the use of the biocompatible amino acid lysine enabled to grow rod-shaped NPs in a flow reactor with a fine control in the optical properties [72].

Au hollow nanostructures were successfully produced in microfluidic systems assisted by the well-known galvanic replacement reaction aforementioned, where the dissolution of a sacrificial NP (template) and the growth of the depositing metal occur at the same time. Then, initial template NPs are required, while the precursor of a second metal is reduced by the sacrificial metal instead of reductants in solution. To proceed with the galvanic replacement reaction, the redox potential of the second metal should be higher than that of the sacrificial metal [73]. A multistage microfluidic system was designed to fine control the production of hollow gold nanoparticles in continuous flow, adding two additional stages to yield nanostructures with optical properties suitable for biomedical applications (Fig. 8.7C) [46]: (1) Co NP synthesis, (2) Au replacement, (3) Co oxidation to release a hollow structure, (4) functionalization with PEG, and (5) sterilization. Spherical-shaped nanoparticles of cobalt were produced in the first stage using a strong reducing agent (NaBH4) that inhibited the oxidation of Co (Fig. 8.7D). The second stage comprised the replacement of Co atoms by Au atoms running the galvanic replacement reaction. Finally, the remaining Co atoms located at the core were oxidized injecting O2 by an O2 slug flow that enables to release hollow gold structures (Fig. 8.7E). This microfluidic procedure enabled a continuous production of Au hollow NPs in only 7min, with a superior productivity than that retrieved from a batch reactor [46].

Ag cubes (Fig. 8.7G) were also electrochemically modified by using the galvanic replacement reaction to render hollow AuAg nanocubes (Fig. 8.7H) [74]. A multiphase liquid-liquid flow microreactor (Fig. 8.7F) was selected as the proper microfluidic system to achieve a more uniform reaction time and a better control on the resulting NPs and on their final optical properties compared to the ones obtained in a batch reactor [74].

SiO2-Au nanoshells were synthesized in continuous flow with a fine control on their morphological properties to achieve the proper optical characteristics to facilitate their biomedical use [75,76]. The formation of SiO2-Au nanoshells required the assembly of three microfluidic units (Fig. 8.7I): synthesis of amino-functionalized silica NPs, seeding with Au NPs, and shell formation by the secondary growth method. The synthesis of this plasmonic nanostructure (Fig. 8.7J) could be successfully achieved in either single-phase flow [75] or multiphase flow [76] due to the fact that the shell growth is not a fast kinetic reaction and it is produced by a secondary growth method. Interestingly, the growth of the Au shell could be modified to render hyperbranched gold structures on silica particles (Fig. 8.7K) [77]. This new nanostructure was produced using the same reagents than those used for synthesizing SiO2-Au nanoshells but modifying the roughness of silica NPs and the nanocrystallization kinetics.

Finally, another secondary growth method was used to produce high-purity Au nanoplates with a well control of thickness [78]. The nanoplate structure was modified to render flat or rolled shapes depending on the residence time used.

As a general rule in nanomaterial synthesis, obtaining monodisperse NPs is of paramount importance to preserve their properties. The synthesis of Ag NPs has been also widely studied in microfluidic reactors, especially in the synthesis of spherical NPs using strong reducing agents such as NaBH4 [79,80]. However, equal to Au NPs, the fast nanocrystallization kinetics of Ag NPs has resulted in a wide NP size distribution and reactor fouling. Different alternatives have been proposed to face those previous drawbacks. Multiphase segmented flow was considered to control the mixing efficiency and to prevent microchannel wall fouling [81]. A coaxial-flow reactor was used to confine the growth of Ag NPs at the interface between the inner and the outer streams preventing reactor fouling [57]. Small 3–5nm Ag NPs were produced in continuous flow without the need of using strong reducing agents; a combination of sodium tripolyphosphate and hydrogen in the segmented flow enabled a controlled growth of Ag NPs just by modifying the reduction rate [82].

Cubic-shaped NPs were generated using a multiphase flow and a secondary growth method, where air was used to form a gas-liquid segmented flow [83]. Air not only promoted a fast mixing due to the internal convective circulation in slugs but also promoted the formation of glycolaldehyde from ethylene glycol and served as buffer space for the diffusion of the resulting NO generated. The concentration of Ag seeds and Ag reagent and the residence time enabled to tune the cube edge length. Ag nanocubes with a monodisperse size distribution were also produced in continuous flow by the sulfide-mediated method, using multiphase segmented flow with the aid of NaHS as a facet-directing specific agent [84]. Finally, a multistage flow microsystem was designed to produce Ag seeds with a multiphase segmented flow and then direct the growth of the seeds toward triangular silver nanoprisms [85]. The edge length of Ag nanoprisms was determined by the amount of the seed NPs introduced to the growth stage and the concentration of available silver ions [85].

Ultrathin Pd nanosheets with a hexagonal shape have unique surface plasmon properties with a strong adsorption in the NIR region and excellent photothermal stability and biocompatibility [86]. To produce this unique nanostructure in continuous flow, a multiphase segmented flow reactor was designed [87], where CO was used as gas-phase carrier to (1) achieve a fast mixing of reagents, (2) reduce Pd2+ ions to Pd0, and (3) control the anisotropic growth (facet-specific capping agent). The shape of nanosheets could be facile tuned by the modification of the synthesis temperature, producing monodisperse nanosheets with a residence time as small as 150s [87]. Triangular nanosheets were produced if the reaction temperature was below 50°C, whereas hexagonal nanoplates were rendered at synthesis temperatures above 90°C [87] (see Fig. 8.8 ).

The production of semiconductor nanomaterials in microfluidics has been extensively studied due to the potential advantages of microsystems over conventional batch approaches. Considering that previous reviews have treated the synthesis of crystalline semiconductors in detail [42,66,67], this section will highlight some works that have produced remarkable results in this topic. The synthesis of II–VI quantum dots such as CdS was first reported to show the benefits of microfluidics [88]. However, the residence time distribution was not good enough to control the mean particle size due to laminar flow limitations, and consequently, multiphase segmented flows were implemented to produce CdSe NPs by liquid-gas [89] and liquid-liquid [90] segmented flows. Considering that quantum dots are usually produced in high-boiling-point solvents at temperatures higher than 250°C, supercritical fluids were successfully tested to suppress the need of using a segmented flow [91]. This alternative is preferred to segmented flow to avoid the most important inconvenience of two-phase flows that is the resulting dependence of the mixing timescale to the corresponding residence time through average flow velocity [60]. However, the use of supercritical fluids requires that the microfluidic system should withstand high pressure (>40bar). The majority of quantum dot research was focused on II–VI systems such as CdSe due to their comparative ease of synthesis, but III–V materials are potentially less toxic and then more convenient for biomedical use. InP NPs were successfully produced in 2min residence time with a continuous-flow process where mixing, aging, and growth were separated to achieve an exquisite control on synthesis conditions [54]. Interestingly, in-line analysis was incorporated to the microfluidic systems to allow monitoring the quality of the NPs in real time [92]. This function was crucial to apply metamodeling algorithms for the controlled synthesis of NPs [93]. Application of such a metamodel allowed the prediction of reaction outcomes with high accuracy and reliability (Fig. 8.9 A).

CuS was produced in continuous flow by a two-stage microsystem and with a single-phase flow [94]. CuS NPs were biodegradable and were formed by a process where first Cu2O NPs were formed (Fig. 8.9B) and then they were modified at high temperature to crystallize into the proper CuS phase of covellite (Fig. 8.9C). This process was based on a Kirkendall diffusion effect, where sulfur diffuses into the Cu2O template particles at the same time that the copper diffuses outward but at a slower rate. As a consequence of the nonreciprocal diffusion between Cu and S, a hollow structure was obtained [94].

Carbon dots are one of the most attractive semiconductor nanomaterials mainly due to their excellent luminescent properties and their high biocompatibility and low-cost synthesis [95]. Carbon dots were first produced in a microfluidic system composed of two modules, one for microfluidics and another for heating and temperature control [96]. The synthesis was based on the thermal decomposition of ascorbic acid in dimethyl sulfoxide (Fig. 8.9D and E) [96].

Iron oxide nanoparticles are of significant interest due to their wide-ranging applications in areas such as drug delivery, hyperthermia treatment, MRI-based contrast agents, and selective separation of biological fluids. These applications require superparamagnetism in the NPs having sizes smaller than 20nm and with a narrow size distribution to ensure uniform physical and chemical properties. Consequently, biomedical applications of iron oxides require the need of improved synthetic methods that are capable of yielding high-quality NPs of uniform size, geometry, and stoichiometry [97]. Iron oxide nanoparticles are usually obtained from a coprecipitation process where an aqueous solution of iron chlorides reacts with a basic solution of NaOH, NH4OH, or tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAOH). Several approaches have been followed to contact the two solutions: single-phase flow, multiphase flow, coaxial flow, and spinning disk processing. Frenz et al. designed a poly(dimethylsiloxane) microchip with microfabricated electrodes, to produce iron oxide NPs in a millisecond mixing time by electrocoalescence of reagent drops [98]. A multiphase flow capillary microreactor was designed to produce 3.6nm dextran-coated superparamagnetic iron oxide NPs [97]. In other cases, a chloride solution was injected coaxially through a silica capillary tubing introduced inside a millifluidic channel where the basic stream was flowing [99]. This synthesis procedure produced a well-defined jet, 3-D centered in the channel. The coaxial jet flow reduced the diffusion lengths, enabled a nucleation far from the channel walls to prevent the microsystem fouling and decreased the mixing times. In addition, the aforementioned parabolic velocity profile, due to the laminar Poiseuille flow, is nearly flat at the center of the focalized jet flow, which ensured a narrow size distribution of iron oxide NPs [99]. Similarly, cobalt ferrite NPs were produced in continuous multistage single-phase flow, separating the mixing and coprecipitation from the aging step [100].

Continuous-flow spinning disk processing (SDP) enabled extremely rapid mixing under plug flow conditions, promoting also effective heat and mass transfers [101]. Superparamagnetic Fe3O4 NPs were produced by passing ammonia gas over a thin aqueous film of Fe2+/3+, which was introduced through a jet feed at the center of the rotating disk. The rotating speed was a key variable to tune the particle size, obtaining larger NPs as the rotating speed was decreased [101]. A commercial glass microreactor was considered to produce iron oxide NPs with a single-phase flow by tailoring the flow rates and localizing the coprecipitation stage to achieve well control in NP size while avoiding channel blockage [102]. This procedure was also applied to produce Fe3O4 NPs doped with Zn to tune the saturation magnetization [103].

Finally, a multiphase gas segmented flow was selected to produce iron oxide NPs with different structure, morphology, and magnetic properties (Fig. 8.10 A) [104]. Iron precursor and alkaline streams were mixed under ultrasound irradiation to favor a fast mixing and homogenous formation of iron hydroxides. Afterward, the aging of hydroxides was produced in a gas-liquid segmented flow. Interestingly, depending on the gas atmosphere used in the segmented flow, an accurate control of the production of Fe3O4 (with H2 or N2) and FeOOH (with CO or O2) was achieved (Fig. 8.10A) [104]. The productivity was enhanced by strongly reducing residence times with respect to the batch process, from hours to minutes or even seconds [104]. Similarly, an ultrasonic tubular microreactor was designed for the continuous-flow precipitation of hydroxyapatite, both in single-phase flow and in multiphase gas-liquid segmented flow (Fig. 8.10B). Hydroxyapatite NPs were obtained using both configurations under near-physiological conditions of pH and temperature, which can be particularly relevant for their application in bone substitution or regeneration [105]. The effect of operating conditions on the mean particle size and on the particle aggregation degree was studied, concluding that both liquid flow rate and flow configurations were strongly affecting the resulting NPs. For instance, the introduction of gas reduced the particle aggregation and decreased their mean size [105]. Hybrid dye-doped SiO2 NPs were produced in continuous flow using a microchannel emulsification technique (Fig. 8.10C) [106]. In this case, a set of two passive micromixers were used to produce the emulsification, and an additional aging unit was added to enable the dye-silica condensation [106]. Batch-type reactors did not allow to decrease the mean particle size under 65nm, but by using this microfluidic system, monodisperse 20nm NPs were produced. The fluorescent quality of the microfluidic-produced NPs was similar to the ones produced in batch, but the productivity was increased 500 times as compared with the reported batch processes, typically conducted in 24h [106].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biomedical Applications of Inorganic nanomaterials Produced by Microfluidics
ce:section.ce:para: In most of the biomedical applications mentioned in Section 1, the nanomaterials of choice are organic in nature and biodegradable. Natural and synthetic polymers and liposomal formulations are used in the majority of those applications because bioaccumulation should be avoided after the NP has accomplished its therapeutic or diagnostic function. However, inorganic NPs can also be applied in several scenarios when their biodegradability is assured (e.g., porous silicon), when their bioaccumulation is reduced, or when they are excreted from the body.

Functional microfluidic channels are also manufactured by incorporating inorganic NPs within or on the building material of the microreactor itself to provide it with catalytic [107], biosensing [108], biological purification [109,110], sorting [111], bioremediation [112], or proteolytic ability [113] to mention just a few applications. However, due to space limitations, all those approaches in which the NPs are separately prepared in a discontinuous batch reactor and integrated in an on-chip biosensor or device will not be reviewed in this chapter. Suggested readings on those approaches are Refs. [114–117]. For the sake of simplicity in the following sections, we will describe several of those inorganic nanomaterials and their demonstrated applications focusing exclusively on those nanomaterials produced using flow microreactors. We will describe exclusively those manuscripts in which the inorganic NPs are produced in a flow reactor and their potential biomedical application validated in the same report.

The sustained ion release from metallic NPs (e.g., silver and copper) is currently used in many consumer products [118] as antimicrobials, acting the NP as a depot that releases cytotoxic ions after oxidation in aerobic media. In the biomedical field, many devices, prosthesis, and implants have been coated with nanosilver in order to control bacteria, yeast, or fungi colonization. Due to the reduced dimensions of silver NPs, their interference with microscale prokaryotic microorganisms and their intracellular components allow multiple mechanisms of action (Fig. 8.11 ). Microbial disinfection is also used thanks to the photocatalytic action of semiconductor NPs (e.g., zinc oxide and titania), which are also commonly used as UV blockers in cosmetics. There are many scientific reports describing the microfluidic synthesis of those materials to fulfill their large demand (e.g., [119]). However, in the biomedical field, the microfluidic synthesis is focused on capping the produced NPs with coatings or stabilizers that minimize their cytotoxic effect against human cells while being effective against prokaryotic cells. In this regard, Chen et al. [120] fabricated microfibers containing silver NPs and graphene oxide embedded in bacterial cellulose using a microfluidic platform showing high antimicrobial action against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria while no cytotoxicity against human liver cells due to a controlled Ag-ion release kinetics. Droplet microfluidics has been used for the fabrication of chitosan microparticles loaded with silver NPs having high antimicrobial effect against Escherichia. coli (DH5α strain) but low cytotoxicity against fibroblasts [121].

Polymeric or liposomal NPs are preferable in drug and gene delivery applications due to their hydrolytic or enzymatic biodegradability, and there are many reports in the literature describing their microfluidic synthesis and their in vitro and even in vivo application of those matrices in drug/gene delivery (e.g., [122,123]). However, the application of microfluidic-synthesized inorganic NPs in this field is limited to porous silicon, mesoporous silica (even though it is not biodegradable), metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), and a few others.

In this regard, Liu et al. [124] have described the microfluidic assembly of porous silicon NPs containing drugs associated with cardiovascular disease with pH-responsive polymeric microparticles containing anti-inflammatory drugs intended for oral delivery. The same group [125] has described the microfluidic assembly using a 3-D microfluidic co-flow-focusing device of porous silicon-based NPs combined with acid-degradable acetalated dextran containing multiple drugs (i.e., sorafenib, paclitaxel, and methotrexate) and the surface functionalization of the resulting hybrid nanocomposites with a cell-penetrating peptide. Hybrid polymeric inorganic/organic NPs prepared by using microfluidics have also been described. In this regard, Yang et al. [126] described the microfluidic-assisted synthesis of multifunctional polycaprolactone microcapsules incorporating CdTe quantum dots, Fe3O4 superparamagnetic NPs, and tamoxifen anticancer drugs using a polydimethylsiloxane chip consisting of three inlet ports, an outlet port, a cross junction, and an observation chamber.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inorganic Nanoparticles in Magnetic Fluid Hyperthermia and in Photothermal Therapy
ce:section.ce:para: There are several examples in the literature in which microfluidics has been used for the continuous synthesis of different plasmonic NPs and their heating efficiency after near-infrared light irradiation corroborated. In this regard, Gomez et al. [46] described the continuous synthesis of hollow gold NPs mentioned before by using galvanic replacement of cobalt NPs also formed in a simple microreactor composed of PEEK-based Y junctions and PTFE tubing. In a subsequential step, the resulting NPs were surface pegylated and downstream sterilized by means of UV light to make them potentially ready for an in vitro or in vivo tests. The NIR absorbance of the resulting particles was corroborated and also their potential application in photothermal therapy. Biodegradable copper sulfide NPs have been prepared in a continuous manner by using two consecutive Y-shaped PEEK micromixers [94]. In the first micromixer, two solutions were interfaced to form the Cu2O seeds, which were reacted with Na2S in a second Y-shaped micromixer to form CuS NPs. NIR absorption in semiconductors such as CuS NPs is a combined effect originated by the SPR and the band-to-band transition of the charge carrier [127]. The heating efficiency of the resulting NPs was evaluated after irradiating them with NIR light (808nm) showing photothermal heating rise after 20 successive laser irradiation cycles (200mW/cm2). A similar photothermal heating was also obtained when using batch- and microfluidic-produced core/shell silica/gold NPs synthesized using a commercial slit interdigital microstructured mixer [75]. However, a considerable reaction-time savings and NP monodispersity were achieved when using the flow reactor.

Habault et al. [128] described the use of glass-based coaxial tubes for the continuous production of magnetic droplets using a simple oil-in-oil emulsion circulating inside an inductor coil producing a radio-frequency magnetic field. Also, functional magnetic NPs (i.e., ferrites and sulfides) have been prepared in a two-stage continuous-flow reactor and the specific loss power of the resulting magnetic NPs evaluated corroborating their potential application in magnetic fluid hyperthermia [129].

The microfluidic synthesis of NPs used as contrast agents has been widely studied. In this regard, Wang et al. [130] have described the continuous synthesis of radiopaque barium sulfate NPs using a flow-focusing microfluidic device. Those authors used alginate microgels containing sulfate ions that were subsequently dripped in a barium-containing solution to promote the alginate cross-linking and barium sulfate precipitation and entrapment. The radiopacity of the resulting nanocomposites was evaluated by means of fluoroscopy.

Photothermal optical coherence tomography (OCT) of 3-D human breast tissue was demonstrated using lysine-functionalized gold nanorods as contrast agents continuously prepared in a PTFE coil rolled in an ultrasonic bath [72].

Dextran-coated superparamagnetic magnetite NPs have also been prepared using a capillary-based droplet-flow reactor and the physicochemical properties of the as-produced NPs as contrast agents evaluated in a 4.7T MRI spectrometer showing r2 relaxativities similar to the ones of commercial iron-based contrast agents (i.e., Feridex) [97]. A dual-imaging technique can be achieved by combining the use of microbubbles used as contrast agents for ultrasound-based imaging with iron oxide NPs used as contrast agents in magnetic resonance imaging. In this regard, Park et al. [131] used soft lithography to prepare a microfluidic flow-focusing bubble generator in which an aqueous solution containing a mixture of lysozyme, alginate, and inorganic NPs including Fe3O4 (or Au or SiO2-encapsulated CdSe/ZnS) was fed through two side channels, whereas a gas (i.e., CO2) was supplied through the inner channel. The inorganic NPs rapidly coated and stabilized the gas bubbles, and an enhanced contrast was demonstrated using an OptiCell chamber after being excited with a focused ultrasound pulse. In addition, an MRI scanner demonstrated also an enhanced negative contrast by increasing the spin-spin relaxation rate compared with the use of bubbles free of magnetic NPs. Superparamagnetic NPs have also been synthesized in a flow reactor composed of two hydrodynamically coupled nozzles being the nanoprecipitation reaction compartmentalized in microdroplets using electrocoalescence to achieve NP monodispersity [98]. The resulting NPs showed high r2 relaxativity that is indicative of their potential use in MR imaging.

A microsegmented flow was used to prepare silver NPs that were subsequently mixed with monomers before a photopolymerization process on the resulting droplets that rendered polyacrylamide/silver composite particles [132]. Those hybrid particles showed strong SERS (i.e., surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy) activity. Gold NPs obtained in a stainless-steel continuous-flow reactor with no reducer or stabilizers were used as SERS substrates in the determination of carboxylated single-walled carbon nanotubes [133].

Enzymatic and sensing ability can be achieved by using microfluidic reactors in the synthesis of enzyme-functionalized silica NPs using PEEK-based Y junctions together with ETFE tubing where the silica hydrolysis and condensation take place at the same time that the enzyme gets immobilized [134]. Those authors demonstrated the enzymatic ability and long-term activity of the resulting NPs using amperometric detection.

Biocompatible MOFs (i.e., MIL-88A) encapsulating functional enzymes (glycerol dehydrogenase, horseradish peroxidase, and acetylcholinesterase) were prepared using a coaxial PFA tubing showing a superior catalytic activity and recyclability than the free enzymes [135]. In addition, those capsules acted as semipermeable membranes preventing the penetration of proteases while preserving the enzyme function.

Continuous microfluidic systems are useful platforms for the reproducible and controllable synthesis of inorganic NPs intended for biomedical use. Microfluidic systems are economical, simple, and powerful tools for the synthesis of nanomaterials with a high control in size, shape, and chemical composition. The flow patterns can be adapted to the type of nanomaterials with the aid of computer fluid dynamic simulations to facilitate the selection of the proper synthesis conditions. Productivity can be scaled up, obtaining the maximum control on synthesis conditions and avoiding the microsystem fouling. Single-phase and multiphase flows are widely applied in the production of noble metal, semiconductor, and oxide NPs. Microfluidic systems can integrate real-time analysis of the generated NPs in order to tune the properties of the produced materials according to the specifications being a major advantage over traditional batch reactors. In addition, microfluidic systems can incorporate automated feedback, using optimization routines, which ease the complex process of nanomaterial synthesis. The integration of multistage processes shows promise for nanoengineering NPs with unique optical, electronic, and magnetic properties. Finally, the expanded synthesis conditions achieved in microfluidic systems and high-pressure and high-temperature conditions enable to reduce the synthesis time usually required in batch reactors and the possibility to nanoengineer new nanomaterials not yet reported. Although it is evidenced that microfluidics improves the nanomaterial production in terms of reaction control and NP quality compared with batch-type reactors, it is also obvious that microfluidic reactors are still in the early stages of development. New procedures based on in-line analysis, multistage chemical reactions, and multiphase flow patterns have been reported to produce complex nanomaterials that contribute to a wide variety of new applications in biomedicine. But new tools such as real-time analysis and control algorithms are still in demand to enable a fast optimization of nanomaterials and promote the translation from laboratory-scale to large-scale production using continuous flow. Then, it implies that currently, microfluidics cannot displace the batch-type reactors as the most important reaction environment for nanomaterial synthesis.

The strong point of nanotechnology is being a multidisciplinary discipline. Different points of view on the same biomedical problem can face it in a synergetic way. Nanomedicine provides new perspectives to overcome clinical challenges thanks to the direct interaction at the cellular and molecular levels of the disease and thanks to the better understanding of the interface between biomolecules and biomaterials. Still, considerable efforts should be dedicated to solve some of the challenges of these nanotechnologies including industrial scale-up, the lack of a global standardization in the nanomaterial characterization methods, long-term validation of the medical outcome, safety evaluation, and demonstrated differential efficacy compared with the current clinical practice.
